name: Time series — fundamentals
description: 25 interview-screen MCQs — time series — fundamentals. Each card explains the answer.
primer:
## Time series — fundamentals
**Weak stationarity** = constant mean/variance and autocovariance depending only on the lag — required for ARMA modelling. **White noise** (zero mean, constant variance, no autocorrelation) is the building block; a **random walk** has a unit root (non-stationary, permanent shocks, variance growing without bound). Test stationarity with **ADF** (H₀: unit root) and **KPSS** (H₀: stationary); difference d times for I(d). Identify orders: AR(p) → PACF cuts off at p, ACF decays; MA(q) → ACF cuts off at q, PACF decays. Use AIC/BIC and check residuals are white noise.

cards.term: A time series is (weakly / covariance) stationary if:
choices: Its mean and variance are constant and autocovariance depends only on the lag
Its mean drifts but its variance stays constant over time
Its distribution is normal at every point in time
It follows a fixed deterministic trend in the mean
Its autocovariance depends on calendar time, not the lag
cards.definition: Weak (covariance) stationarity requires a constant mean, a constant variance, and an autocovariance that depends only on the lag separation, not on calendar time; this stable structure is what lets the autocorrelation function be estimated and used. A drifting mean violates the constant-mean condition outright. A normal distribution is neither necessary nor sufficient: stationarity is about moments and autocovariance, not the shape of the distribution. A deterministic trend means the mean changes with time, again breaking the constant-mean requirement. Autocovariance that depends on calendar time rather than only the lag is precisely the non-stationary case.

cards.term: Most standard time-series models (ARMA) assume the series is:
choices: Cointegrated with another series
Normally distributed only
Stationary
Trending upward
Non-stationary
cards.definition: ARMA models assume stationarity because their fixed AR and MA coefficients only make sense when the autocorrelation structure is stable over time, which is what stationarity guarantees. Cointegration is a relationship between two non-stationary series, not an assumption of a univariate ARMA fit. Requiring only normality confuses the distribution of the errors with the second-moment stability that ARMA actually needs. A trending or non-stationary series violates the constant-mean and finite-variance conditions, so it must be differenced or detrended before ARMA can apply.

cards.term: White noise is a process with:
choices: Strong positive serial correlation at short lags
Zero mean, constant variance, and no autocorrelation
A unit root and permanent shocks
Variance that grows linearly with time
A constant nonzero mean trend
cards.definition: White noise has zero mean, constant variance, and zero autocorrelation at all non-zero lags - it is the irreducible random component, and a well-fitting model should leave residuals that look like it. Strong serial correlation is precisely what white noise lacks, since each observation is uncorrelated with its past. A unit root with permanent shocks describes a non-stationary random walk, whereas white noise is stationary. Variance growing with time again describes a random walk, not constant-variance white noise. A constant nonzero mean trend would break the zero-mean condition white noise requires.

cards.term: A random walk, $y_t = y_{t-1} + e_t$, is:
choices: Stationary with constant, bounded variance
Trend-stationary once a line is removed
Mean-reverting toward a fixed level
Non-stationary, with variance that grows over time
White noise drawn independently each period
cards.definition: A random walk has a unit root, so shocks are never dampened: each innovation is permanently accumulated and the variance grows linearly with time, making it non-stationary. It is therefore not stationary with bounded variance - the variance is unbounded. It is not trend-stationary, because removing a deterministic line cannot fix a stochastic trend driven by a unit root. It does not mean-revert; with coefficient exactly one there is no pull back to any level. It is not white noise - it is the cumulative sum of white-noise increments, not the increments themselves.

cards.term: The best forecast of a pure random walk for the next period is:
choices: The current (most recent) value
Zero, the unconditional mean
The simple average of all past values
An exponentially weighted average of the past
The long-run mean it reverts to
cards.definition: For a pure random walk the increments are unpredictable white noise, so the conditional expectation of the next value equals the current value; this remains the optimal forecast at every horizon. Zero would only be optimal for a zero-mean stationary series, which a random walk is not. The simple average of all past values and the long-run mean both assume mean reversion to a fixed level, which a unit-root process lacks. An exponentially weighted average smooths past data, but for a random walk only the most recent observation carries the relevant information.

cards.term: A unit root in a time series implies:
choices: The variance stays constant over time
The series mean-reverts quickly to its average
Non-stationarity, with shocks that have permanent effects
The series is stationary around its mean
There is no autocorrelation at any lag
cards.definition: A unit root - a root of the AR characteristic polynomial equal to one - makes the level wander without returning to a fixed mean, so the series is non-stationary and shocks have permanent effects. The variance is therefore not constant; it grows over time. The series does not mean-revert quickly; it does not mean-revert at all. Saying it is stationary is the direct contradiction of having a unit root. Lack of autocorrelation describes white noise, the opposite of the strong persistence a unit root produces.

cards.term: The null hypothesis of the Augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF) test is:
choices: The two series are not cointegrated
The series is stationary around its mean
The series has a unit root (it is non-stationary)
The model errors are normally distributed
The series is white noise with no structure
cards.definition: The ADF null hypothesis is that the series contains a unit root and is therefore non-stationary; you reject it (concluding stationarity) only when the test statistic is sufficiently negative. The no-cointegration null belongs to cointegration tests like Engle-Granger, not ADF. Stationarity is the KPSS null and the ADF alternative, not its null. Normality of errors and white-noise behaviour are separate diagnostic properties, not what ADF is testing.

cards.term: The KPSS test differs from the ADF test in that its null hypothesis is:
choices: A unit root, exactly matching ADF's null
Normality of the model residuals
No cointegration between two series
Stationarity (the opposite of ADF's null)
No autocorrelation in the residuals
cards.definition: KPSS reverses the testing setup: its null hypothesis is that the series is stationary (or trend-stationary), the opposite of ADF's unit-root null, which is why the two are run together as a cross-check. A unit-root null would just duplicate ADF, not complement it. Normality and no autocorrelation are unrelated diagnostic hypotheses. No cointegration is the hypothesis of cointegration tests, not of KPSS. Because the nulls are flipped, agreement between ADF rejection and KPSS non-rejection gives a stronger stationarity conclusion.

cards.term: To make a series with a unit root stationary, the usual transformation is to:
choices: Take first differences of the series
Add a deterministic time trend
Take logarithms of the levels only
Standardise it to mean zero, unit variance
Square each value of the series
cards.definition: First differencing removes a stochastic (unit-root) trend, and a series that needs d differences to become stationary is said to be integrated of order d, I(d). Adding a time trend addresses a deterministic trend, not the stochastic trend a unit root creates. Taking logs alone stabilises variance or linearises exponential growth but does not remove a unit root. Standardising only rescales mean and variance, leaving the non-stationary dynamics intact. Squaring distorts the series without eliminating the unit root.

cards.term: A trend-stationary series is one that becomes stationary after:
choices: Adding a unit root to the dynamics
Taking logs of the raw levels
Removing a deterministic trend (detrending)
Adjusting only for the seasonal pattern
Taking first differences of the series
cards.definition: A trend-stationary series becomes stationary once a deterministic trend is removed (detrending), because its non-stationarity comes from a fixed function of time rather than a unit root. Adding a unit root would make it more non-stationary, not less. Taking logs only rescales and does not remove a trend. Seasonal adjustment handles periodic patterns, a different problem. First differencing is the correct fix for a stochastic trend, but applied to a trend-stationary series it overdifferences and injects spurious MA structure.

cards.term: In an ARIMA(p, d, q) model, the parameter d denotes:
choices: The number of periods ahead forecast
The number of moving-average lags
The number of autoregressive lags
The number of differences taken to reach stationarity
The length of the seasonal period
cards.definition: In ARIMA(p, d, q), d is the number of times the series is differenced to reach stationarity, i.e. the order of integration. The forecast horizon is a property of the prediction step, not a model parameter. The number of moving-average lags is q, and the number of autoregressive lags is p - these are the other two ARIMA orders, not d. The seasonal period belongs to the seasonal specification of a SARIMA model, not to the d term.

cards.term: An ARIMA(0,1,0) model is equivalent to:
choices: An MA(1) process with a single error lag
An AR(1) process with a single level lag
White noise needing no differencing at all
A random walk (a unit-root I(1) process)
A purely deterministic linear time trend
cards.definition: ARIMA(0,1,0) has no AR or MA terms and one difference (d=1), so the differenced series is white noise - i.e. the level is a random walk, $y_t = y_{t-1} + e_t$. It is not an AR(1) or MA(1) (those set p=1 or q=1), not undifferenced white noise (d=0), and not a deterministic trend (a random walk's trend is stochastic).

cards.term: For a stationary AR(1) process $y_t = \phi y_{t-1} + e_t$, stationarity requires:
choices: $\phi$ strictly greater than one
$\phi$ somewhere between one and two
$\phi$ equal to exactly zero only
$\phi$ equal to exactly one (a unit root)
the absolute value of $\phi$ to be below one
cards.definition: An AR(1) process is stationary if and only if the absolute value of the coefficient is strictly less than one, so that shocks decay geometrically and the series mean-reverts. A coefficient greater than one makes the process explosive, and a value between one and two is still outside the stationary region. Requiring it to equal zero only describes white noise, far narrower than the stationarity condition. A coefficient of exactly one is the borderline unit-root case, which gives a random walk and is non-stationary.

cards.term: A moving-average MA(q) process is:
choices: Never invertible for any choice of q
Equivalent to a random walk
Stationary only when it has a unit root
Always stationary (for finite q)
Always non-stationary regardless of q
cards.definition: A finite-order MA(q) process is always stationary because it is a fixed finite linear combination of white-noise terms, giving constant mean and variance and autocovariances that vanish beyond lag q. It is not always non-stationary - that contradicts this construction. It is not equivalent to a random walk, which is an infinite cumulative sum with a unit root. It does not need a unit root to be stationary; a unit root would make it non-stationary. And invertibility (recovering the errors from past observations) holds when the MA roots lie outside the unit circle, so it is not never invertible.

cards.term: To identify the order of a pure AR(p) process from sample plots, you look for:
choices: A PACF that decays slowly across lags
A flat ACF at every lag
A unit root visible in the PACF
A PACF that cuts off after lag p
An ACF that cuts off after lag p
cards.definition: A pure AR(p) process is identified by a PACF that cuts off (drops to zero) after lag p, while its ACF tails off gradually - the PACF isolates the direct lag-p effect. A slowly decaying PACF is not the AR signature; it is the ACF that decays for an AR process. A flat ACF at all lags describes white noise, not AR structure. There is no such diagnostic as a unit root in the PACF. An ACF that cuts off after lag p is the MA(q) signature, the mirror image of the AR pattern.

cards.term: To identify the order of a pure MA(q) process, you look for:
choices: A PACF that cuts off after lag q
A flat PACF at every lag
An ACF that cuts off after lag q
A unit root in the differenced series
An ACF that decays slowly across lags
cards.definition: A pure MA(q) process is identified by an ACF that cuts off after lag q, because observations more than q periods apart share no common white-noise term; its PACF tails off gradually. A PACF cutting off after lag q is the AR signature, the reverse pattern. A flat PACF at all lags is not the MA diagnostic. A unit root signals non-stationarity, unrelated to MA order identification. An ACF that decays slowly is the AR pattern, not the sharp ACF cutoff that marks an MA process.

cards.term: AIC and BIC are used in time-series modelling to:
choices: Test two series for cointegration
Detect heteroskedasticity in the errors
Measure the bias of forecasts
Select model order, trading fit against complexity
Test the series for a unit root
cards.definition: AIC and BIC are information criteria used to select model order by balancing goodness of fit against a penalty for the number of parameters, with BIC penalising complexity more heavily and tending to pick smaller models. They do not test for cointegration - that is the role of cointegration tests. They do not detect heteroskedasticity, which calls for tests like ARCH-LM. They measure penalised in-sample likelihood, not forecast bias. And they are not unit-root tests like ADF or KPSS.

cards.term: The autocorrelation function (ACF) at lag k measures:
choices: The slope of the series' time trend
The correlation between two different series
The correlation between observations k periods apart
The variance of the series at time k
The forecast error at horizon k
cards.definition: The autocorrelation function at lag k measures the correlation between observations k periods apart in the same series, summarising its linear dependence on its own past. It is not the trend slope, which is a regression coefficient against time. It is not the correlation between two different series - that is cross-correlation. It is not the variance at time k; the ACF is a normalised correlation, not a variance. Nor is it the forecast error at horizon k, which is a prediction concept rather than a property of the data's own dependence.

cards.term: The partial autocorrelation at lag k measures the correlation between $y_t$ and $y_{t-k}$:
choices: Including all the intervening lags
After removing the effect of the intervening lags
Of the squared series, not the level
At the seasonal frequency only
Between two separate series, not one
cards.definition: The partial autocorrelation at lag k measures the correlation between the series and its value k periods earlier after removing the effect of all the intervening lags, isolating the direct relationship. Including all intervening lags instead describes the ordinary ACF, which does not control for them. The squared series relates to volatility diagnostics, not the level PACF. The seasonal frequency is a special lag, not the general definition. Correlation between two separate series is cross-correlation, not partial autocorrelation.

cards.term: The Ljung-Box test applied to model residuals checks whether:
choices: The residuals are heteroskedastic
The series has a unit root
The residuals are normally distributed
The residuals show remaining autocorrelation
Two series are cointegrated
cards.definition: The Ljung-Box test on residuals checks whether any autocorrelation remains across a set of lags; a well-specified model should leave residuals indistinguishable from white noise. It does not test heteroskedasticity, which needs ARCH-type tests. It does not test for a unit root - that is ADF or KPSS. It does not test normality, which calls for tests like Jarque-Bera. And it concerns a single residual series, not cointegration between two series.

cards.term: Seasonality in a monthly series is often handled with:
choices: Seasonal differencing or a SARIMA model
Ordinary first differencing only
A logit model on the levels
Heteroskedasticity-robust standard errors
An instrumental-variable regression
cards.definition: Monthly seasonality is handled with seasonal differencing or a SARIMA model, which adds AR, differencing, and MA terms at the seasonal lag (12 for monthly data) to capture the repeating pattern. Plain first differencing removes a trend but not the period-12 seasonal cycle. A logit model is for binary outcomes, irrelevant here. Robust standard errors only correct inference for heteroskedasticity or autocorrelation; they do not model seasonality. An instrumental variable addresses endogeneity, a different problem entirely.

cards.term: Out-of-sample forecast evaluation is important because:
choices: It serves as a unit-root test
In-sample fit is always the lower number
It strips seasonality from the data
In-sample fit can be inflated by overfitting
It guarantees the series is stationary
cards.definition: Out-of-sample evaluation matters because in-sample fit can be inflated by overfitting - a model can match past noise and overstate its true predictive ability - so holding out data gives an honest forecast assessment. It is not a unit-root test. The claim that in-sample fit is always lower is backwards; in-sample fit is typically higher than out-of-sample. Out-of-sample testing does not strip out seasonality, which is a modelling step. And it cannot guarantee stationarity, which is a property of the data, not of the evaluation method.

cards.term: An AR(1) process with $\phi = 0.95$ is best described as:
choices: White noise with no persistence
Stationary but highly persistent (slow mean reversion)
A random walk with a unit root
Anti-persistent and oscillating in sign
Non-stationary and explosive over time
cards.definition: An AR(1) with coefficient 0.95 is stationary (since the absolute coefficient is below one) but highly persistent: shocks decay slowly, so mean reversion is sluggish and the series behaves almost like a random walk. It is not white noise, which would have a coefficient near zero and no persistence. It is not literally a random walk, which needs a coefficient of exactly one. It is not anti-persistent or oscillating, which would require a negative coefficient. And it is not non-stationary or explosive, because the coefficient remains strictly inside the unit circle.

cards.term: Differencing a series that is actually trend-stationary (overdifferencing) can:
choices: Introduce a non-invertible moving-average structure
Remove heteroskedasticity from the errors
Guarantee strictly better forecasts
Always improve the fitted model
Create a genuine unit root in the levels
cards.definition: Overdifferencing a trend-stationary series introduces an artificial, often non-invertible moving-average structure into the errors, harming estimation and forecasts. It does not remove heteroskedasticity, which is a variance issue unrelated to differencing. It does not guarantee or always improve the model; the opposite, it degrades it when the trend was deterministic. And it does not create a unit root in levels - if anything, differencing a stationary series creates a unit root in the MA polynomial, the mirror problem. The correct treatment depends on whether the trend is deterministic (detrend) or stochastic (difference).

cards.term: The lag operator L applied to a series satisfies:
choices: $Ly_t$ equals the mean of $y$
$Ly_t$ equals the first difference of $y$
$Ly_t$ equals $y_t$ squared
cards.definition: The lag (backshift) operator $L$ maps a series to its value one period earlier, so $Ly_t = y_{t-1}$; this underlies compact ARIMA polynomial notation. It does not return the mean of y, which is a summary statistic, not a shift. It does not give the first difference - that is (1 minus L) times y_t, a combination involving L, not L itself. It does not shift forward to y_{t+1}; that would be the lead operator. And it does not square the series, which is a nonlinear transformation rather than a time shift.
